content.introduction: Hash tables provide average constant-time key lookup by mapping keys to buckets through hash functions.

sections.title: 6.3.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define hashing, buckets, load factor, and collision concepts. Explain why uniform key distribution matters.
examples: Map string keys to bucket indices with a hash function
Explain collision occurrence with finite bucket count

sections.title: 6.3.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe collision resolution methods (chaining, open addressing) and resizing/rehashing workflow.
examples: Trace linear probing sequence after collision
Trigger resize when load factor threshold is exceeded

sections.title: 6.3.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: Average operations are O(1), with worst-case O(n). Discuss practical tuning via load factor and hash quality.
examples: Compare memory overhead of chaining vs probing
Estimate performance degradation under poor hashes
Choose hash table for frequent exact key lookups

keyTakeaways: Understand hash mapping and collision handling
Implement core operations under chosen strategy
Tune load factor and resizing policy
Reason about average vs worst-case complexity
Apply hash tables in key-value intensive services
